title: Penguin Chicks Take the Plunge
story: Penguins are known for their waddling, but on April 22, 2025, they became high-flying stars. National Geographic released a groundbreaking documentary called 'Secrets of the Penguins.' The film captured an incredible sight: baby penguins leaping from a towering cliff for their very first swim. Using drones, filmmakers recorded these brave chicks taking a heart-stopping plunge. It was the first time such footage had ever been captured, giving viewers a bird's-eye view of this amazing natural spectacle.
activity_question: What was special about the penguin footage in the documentary?
activity_choices: It was the first drone footage of penguin chicks jumping
It featured penguins dancing on ice
activity_answer: It was the first drone footage of penguin chicks jumping
follow_up_question: Why do you think penguin chicks need to jump from such a high cliff for their first swim?

title: Moon Buggy Adventure
story: Zoom! Astronauts John Young and Charles Duke took the coolest ride ever on April 22, 1972. They weren't in a car or on a bike. These brave space explorers drove a special vehicle called the Lunar Rover across the Moon's dusty surface. Part of NASA's Apollo 16 mission, this lunar road trip let them explore farther than any astronauts before. Imagine cruising where no one had ever driven, with Earth hanging in the sky!

follow_up_question: Why do you think a rover was important for exploring the Moon?

title: Sweet Success: Denmark's Giant Lollipop
story: Candy lovers, prepare to be amazed! In 1994, a Danish candy factory called BonBon created something extraordinary. On April 22, they unveiled a massive lollipop that tipped the scales at 3,011 pounds. This colossal confection became the world's largest lollipop at the time. Imagine a sweet treat bigger than a car! While this sugary record has since been surpassed, it remains a testament to human creativity and our love for oversized candy.

title: The Vanishing Star Explosion
story: A dazzling light show lit up the night sky. Stargazers marveled at a brilliant new star, shining brighter than Venus. This cosmic spectacle was actually a massive exploding star, now known as the Crab Nebula supernova. For nearly three weeks, it was visible to the naked eye, even during daylight. On April 22, 1056, it faded from view, becoming the last time humans could see a supernova without telescopes.
activity_question: What was the cosmic spectacle that lit up the night sky?
bonus_fact: The Crab Nebula is still expanding today, growing about 1 billion miles wider every day!

title: Sailing Solo Around the World
story: Waves crashed and winds howled as Robin Knox-Johnston steered his boat into harbor. After 312 thrilling days at sea, he had just made history. On April 22, 1969, Knox-Johnston became the first person ever to sail around the entire world alone, without stopping. His incredible journey took him through storms, icebergs, and lonely nights under starry skies. This brave sailor proved that with determination, one person could conquer the vast oceans.

Cross the Atlantic Ocean in a rowboat
Discover a new island in the Pacific

title: Rolling into History: The Volito Skate
story: Wheels of fun spun into action when British inventor Robert John Tyers created something special. On April 22, 1823, he patented an exciting new toy: the Volito. His invention rolled us closer to the inline skates we know today!

title: Space Station Docking Drama
story: Blast off! In 1971, Soyuz 10 roared into the sky on April 22, aiming to be the first spacecraft to dock with a space station. The cosmonauts were excited to visit Salyut 1, the world's first orbiting laboratory. But space exploration isn't always smooth sailing. Despite their best efforts, the docking attempt failed. The disappointed crew had to turn back, returning safely to Earth without stepping foot on the pioneering station.

title: Basketball's Game-Changing Clock
story: Swish! The NBA scored big in 1954 when it introduced two rules that sped up the game. On April 22 that year, basketball officials approved the 24-second shot clock. activity_question: What happened to the Soyuz T-8 spacecraft?
follow_up_question: Why do you think it's important for astronauts to be prepared for unexpected problems in space?

title: Art Takes Over Venice
story: Artists flocked to Venice, Italy, for an exciting new event in 1894. The first Venice Biennale opened its doors on April 22, showcasing the best Italian art. This grand exhibition would happen every two years, bringing creativity and culture to the beautiful canal city. It quickly became a must-see for art lovers from around the world.
activity_question: How often would the Venice Biennale happen?
bonus_fact: Biennale means every two years in Italian. There are now biennales for art, music, and more worldwide!

title: The UK's Phone Number Makeover
story: Ring, ring! In 2000, the UK gave its phone system a big upgrade. On April 22, millions of phone numbers across the country changed overnight. This massive update, called 'The Big Number Change', helped create space for more phone lines as technology grew. People had to remember new area codes and update their contact lists!
activity_question: What was the name of the UK's phone number update?
follow_up_question: Why do you think countries need to update their phone systems?

title: A Natural Wonder Opens in Amsterdam
story: Butterflies fluttered and exotic plants bloomed as the Conservatory of Nature Monument in Amsterdam opened its doors. On April 22, 1905, this green oasis in the heart of the city began welcoming visitors. The conservatory showcased a stunning collection of plants and insects from around the world, giving people a chance to explore nature's wonders without leaving town.
activity_question: What opened in Amsterdam in 1905?
follow_up_question: Why do you think conservatories are important for cities?
